signatures.label: SELECT column1, column2 FROM table_name WHERE condition

documentation.value: Retrieves rows from one or more tables. The most commonly used SQL command in PostgreSQL.
The columns to retrieve. Use * for all columns.
The table or view to query from.
Optional WHERE clause to filter results.

signatures.label: INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2) VALUES (value1, value2) RETURNING *

documentation.value: Inserts new rows into a table. PostgreSQL supports RETURNING clause to return inserted rows.
The target table.
The columns to insert values into.
The values to insert.

signatures.label: UPDATE table_name SET column1 = value1 WHERE condition RETURNING *

documentation.value: Modifies existing rows in a table. Supports RETURNING to return the updated rows.
The table to update.
The column-value assignments.
The WHERE clause to identify rows to update.

signatures.label: CREATE TABLE table_name (column1 type, column2 type, ...)

documentation.value: Creates a new table in the database. Supports PostgreSQL-specific types like SERIAL, JSONB, UUID, ARRAY.
The name of the new table (optionally schema-qualified).
Column definitions with name and type (e.g., id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, data JSONB).

signatures.label: ALTER TABLE table_name ADD COLUMN column_name type

documentation.value: Modifies an existing table definition. Supports ADD/DROP COLUMN, RENAME, SET DEFAULT, constraints.
The table to alter.
The name of the column to add.
The PostgreSQL data type for the new column.
Returns the first non-NULL argument. Often used to provide default values for nullable columns.
The first expression to evaluate.
The fallback expression if value1 is NULL.
Returns NULL if value1 equals value2; otherwise returns value1. Useful to avoid division-by-zero.
The value to return if not equal to value2.
The value to compare against.
Aggregate function that collects all input values into an array.
The expression to aggregate into an array.
Concatenates input values into a string separated by a delimiter.
The text expression to aggregate.
The separator string placed between values.
Builds a JSON object from a variadic argument list of alternating keys and values.
The key for the first entry (must be text).
The value for the first entry (any SQL type).
The key for the second entry.
The value for the second entry.
Converts a timestamp, interval, integer, or numeric value to a string using a format pattern.
The value to format (timestamp, interval, numeric).
The format pattern (e.g., 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS', 'FM999,999.00').
Truncates a timestamp or interval to the specified precision.
The precision: 'microseconds', 'milliseconds', 'second', 'minute', 'hour', 'day', 'week', 'month', 'quarter', 'year'.
The timestamp or interval value to truncate.
Generates a series of values from start to stop with an optional step. Works with integer, bigint, and timestamp types.
The start value of the series.
The end value of the series (inclusive).
Optional step increment. Defaults to 1 for integers or '1 day' for timestamps.
Returns all captured substrings from a POSIX regular expression match.
The string to match against.
The POSIX regular expression pattern.
Optional flags: 'g' (global), 'i' (case-insensitive), 'n' (newline-sensitive).
